PEOPLE.question: Do all developers receive annual training on the OWASP Top 10?
PEOPLE.yes_attribute: All developers receive annual training on the OWASP Top 10.
PEOPLE.no_attribute: Not all developers receive annual training on the OWASP Top 10.
remediation: Select a reputable third-party training platform or develop an internal curriculum covering the current OWASP Top 10 vulnerabilities.
Implement a tracking system (LMS) to ensure 100% completion of training for all engineering staff on an annual basis.
Make OWASP training a mandatory prerequisite for new developer onboarding and production code access.

PEOPLE.question: Is there a documented process for customers to report product security issues?
PEOPLE.yes_attribute: There is a documented process for customers to report product security issues.
PEOPLE.no_attribute: There is no documented process for customers to report product security issues.
remediation: Create a public security policy or 'security.txt' file that clearly outlines the channels for reporting vulnerabilities.
Establish an internal triage workflow with defined response times to acknowledge and validate customer-reported security bugs.
Automate the ticketing process for security reports to ensure they are immediately routed to the security team.

PEOPLE.question: Is role-specific security training (e.g., cloud security, secure APIs) required for senior engineers?
PEOPLE.yes_attribute: Role-specific security training (e.g., cloud security, secure APIs) is required for senior engineers.
PEOPLE.no_attribute: Role-specific security training (e.g., cloud security, secure APIs) is not required for senior engineers.
remediation: Identify key technical specializations (cloud, mobile, API) and map advanced security training modules to corresponding senior roles.
Allocate dedicated professional development budget for senior engineers to obtain industry-recognized security certifications.
Integrate advanced security training milestones into the career development plans for senior and lead engineering positions.

PEOPLE.question: Is there a dedicated technical support tier for handling customer security and privacy inquiries?
PEOPLE.yes_attribute: There is a dedicated technical support tier for handling customer security and privacy inquiries.
PEOPLE.no_attribute: There is no dedicated technical support tier for handling customer security and privacy inquiries.
remediation: Define the organizational structure and required expertise for a specialized security support team to handle complex inquiries.
Document standard operating procedures (SOPs) for escalating security and privacy tickets from general support to the specialized tier.
Provide regular training for specialized support staff on the product’s security features and data privacy architecture.

PEOPLE.question: Is security performance (e.g., low bug density) used as a KPI for engineering promotions?
PEOPLE.yes_attribute: Security performance (e.g., low bug density) is used as a KPI for engineering promotions.
PEOPLE.no_attribute: Security performance (e.g., low bug density) is not used as a KPI for engineering promotions.
remediation: Establish clear, measurable security metrics such as 'security bug density' or 'mean time to remediate' (MTTR) for developers.
Update the engineering career ladder and review templates to explicitly include security contributions and code quality.
Provide managers with dashboards to track team and individual adherence to secure coding standards during promotion cycles.

PEOPLE.question: Does the product team provide "security office hours" or proactive guidance to help customers harden their specific instances?
PEOPLE.yes_attribute: The product team provides "security office hours" or proactive guidance to help customers harden their specific instances.
PEOPLE.no_attribute: The product team does not provide "security office hours" or proactive guidance to help customers harden their specific instances.
remediation: Schedule recurring 'security office hours' led by security architects to address customer-specific hardening questions.
Publish a comprehensive 'security hardening guide' with actionable checklists for customers to optimize their deployment.
Develop automated 'health check' scripts that customers can run to identify potential misconfigurations in their own environments.

PROCESS.question: Are basic security gates (code reviews) required for all releases?
PROCESS.yes_attribute: Basic security gates (code reviews) are required for all releases.
PROCESS.no_attribute: Basic security gates (code reviews) are not required for all releases.
remediation: Implement a mandatory peer review policy for all code changes before they can be merged into the main branch.
Configure branch protection rules in your version control system (e.g., GitHub/GitLab) to require at least one approval from a designated reviewer.
Utilize a standardized code review checklist that includes specific security-focused items like input validation, error handling, and authorization checks.

PROCESS.question: Is there a public-facing contact for vulnerability reporting?
PROCESS.yes_attribute: There is a public-facing contact for vulnerability reporting.
PROCESS.no_attribute: There is no public-facing contact for vulnerability reporting.
remediation: Setup a dedicated security email address (e.g., security@yourcompany.com) or a secure web form for external researchers to report issues.
Publish a clear vulnerability disclosure policy (VDP) on the company website that outlines legal safe harbor and reporting expectations.
Implement a 'security.txt' file in the root directory of the product's domain to help security researchers easily discover your reporting channels.

PROCESS.question: Are critical vulnerabilities patched within 30 days of discovery?
PROCESS.yes_attribute: Critical vulnerabilities are patched within 30 days of discovery.
PROCESS.no_attribute: Critical vulnerabilities are not patched within 30 days of discovery.
remediation: Define and document a formal patch management policy with specific SLAs for different severity levels (e.g., critical = 30 days).
Integrate automated vulnerability scanning tools that alert the engineering team immediately upon discovery of a critical flaw.
Establish a recurring 'Patch Tuesday' or similar maintenance window to ensure the regular deployment of security fixes across all environments.

PROCESS.question: Is a formal s-SDLC followed for every sprint, including threat modeling?
PROCESS.yes_attribute: A formal s-SDLC is followed for every sprint, including threat modeling.
PROCESS.no_attribute: A formal s-SDLC is not followed for every sprint.
remediation: Formalize the secure software development life cycle (s-SDLC) and integrate it into the standard sprint planning process for all squads.
Conduct mandatory threat modeling sessions at the start of any major feature design phase to identify potential architectural risks early.
Train 'security champions' within each engineering squad to facilitate threat modeling and secure design reviews locally.

PROCESS.question: Is there a formal bug bounty program with defined triage and remediation SLAs?
PROCESS.yes_attribute: There is a formal bug bounty program with defined triage and remediation SLAs.
PROCESS.no_attribute: There is no formal bug bounty program.
remediation: Launch a private bug bounty program on a platform like HackerOne or Bugcrowd to start receiving vetted reports from trusted researchers.
Define clear internal service level agreements (SLAs) for the triage, validation, and resolution of reported security bugs.
Transition from a private to a public bug bounty program once internal security maturity and remediation capacity can handle the volume.

PROCESS.question: Is there a risk-prioritization process (CVSS/EPSS) to manage the vulnerability backlog?
PROCESS.yes_attribute: A risk-prioritization process (CVSS/EPSS) is used to manage the vulnerability backlog.
PROCESS.no_attribute: No risk-prioritization process is in place for the vulnerability backlog.
remediation: Adopt the CVSS (Common Vulnerability Scoring System) framework to consistently measure and rank the severity of identified vulnerabilities.
Incorporate EPSS (Exploit Prediction Scoring System) data into the prioritization process to focus on vulnerabilities most likely to be exploited in the wild.
Maintain a centralized vulnerability backlog and conduct weekly reviews to adjust priorities based on emerging threat intelligence and business context.

PROCESS.question: Are security gates fully automated in the CI/CD pipeline, blocking non-compliant builds?
PROCESS.yes_attribute: Security gates are fully automated in the CI/CD pipeline and block non-compliant builds.
PROCESS.no_attribute: Security gates are not fully automated or do not block non-compliant builds.
remediation: Integrate static application security testing (SAST) and software composition analysis (SCA) directly into the CI/CD pipeline.
Set policy threshold levels that automatically fail and block the build if high or critical vulnerabilities are detected during the CI stage.
Automate the deployment and execution of dynamic application security testing (DAST) in a staging environment before any production release.

PROCESS.question: Are RCA findings from public reports fed back into the SDLC to prevent systemic recurrence?
PROCESS.yes_attribute: RCA findings from public reports are fed back into the SDLC.
PROCESS.no_attribute: RCA findings are not systematically fed back into the SDLC.
remediation: Conduct a formal root cause analysis (RCA) for every major security incident or high-severity bug bounty report received.
Create a 'lessons learned' knowledge base and update secure coding guidelines based on the findings from previous RCAs.
Implement automated linting rules or custom static analysis checks specifically designed to prevent the recurrence of previously identified flaw patterns.

PROCESS.question: Is there an automated rollback or "emergency patch" process for critical production flaws?
PROCESS.yes_attribute: An automated rollback or emergency patch process exists for critical production flaws.
PROCESS.no_attribute: No automated rollback or emergency patch process exists.
remediation: Develop and document a high-speed 'emergency patch' workflow that bypasses non-critical staging steps for verified production flaws.
Implement automated rollback capabilities in the deployment pipeline to instantly revert to a known-good state if a flaw is discovered post-release.
Conduct quarterly 'fire drills' or simulations of the emergency patch process to ensure the team can execute a deployment within hours of discovery.

TECHNOLOGY.question: Are customer data sets logically separated at the database or application layer?
TECHNOLOGY.yes_attribute: Customer data sets are logically separated at the database or application layer.
TECHNOLOGY.no_attribute: Customer data sets are not logically separated between tenants.
remediation: Implement a 'tenant ID' column on every database table containing customer data and enforce its use in all application-level queries.
Utilize row-level security (RLS) features within your database engine to automatically restrict data access based on the authenticated user's tenant context.
Perform regular automated testing (e.g., negative tests) to verify that a request for one tenant's ID using another tenant's session returns an error.

TECHNOLOGY.question: Does the product generate basic logs for successful/failed logins?
TECHNOLOGY.yes_attribute: The product generates basic logs for successful and failed login attempts.
TECHNOLOGY.no_attribute: The product does not generate logs for login attempts.
remediation: Enable logging for all authentication events, ensuring you capture the timestamp, source IP address, username, and success/fail status.
Ensure that login logs are written to a secure, write-only log stream that is separate from standard application debug logs.
Review logging configurations to ensure that sensitive data like passwords or full session tokens are never recorded in plaintext.

TECHNOLOGY.question: Does the product support basic username/password complexity?
TECHNOLOGY.yes_attribute: The product supports and enforces basic username/password complexity requirements.
TECHNOLOGY.no_attribute: The product does not enforce password complexity requirements.
remediation: Implement server-side validation to enforce minimum password length and a variety of character types (uppercase, lowercase, numbers, symbols).
Integrate a library to check passwords against common 'pwned' password databases or 'leaked credential' lists during account creation or password resets.
Provide real-time feedback to users in the UI regarding password strength to encourage more secure choices during the sign-up process.

TECHNOLOGY.question: Are API keys and database credentials encrypted in a config file?
TECHNOLOGY.yes_attribute: API keys and database credentials are encrypted within configuration files.
TECHNOLOGY.no_attribute: API keys and credentials are stored in plaintext in configuration files.
remediation: Use a tool like SOPs or ansible vault to encrypt sensitive configuration files before committing them to your version control repository.
Implement a build-time process that injects secrets from an environment variable rather than hardcoding them into configuration files.
Utilize public-key encryption so that only the target production server's private key can decrypt the application configuration at startup.

TECHNOLOGY.question: Is there automated validation to ensure one customer cannot access another’s data (IDOR prevention)?
TECHNOLOGY.yes_attribute: Automated validation is in place to prevent insecure direct object reference (IDOR) attacks.
TECHNOLOGY.no_attribute: There is no automated validation to prevent one customer from accessing another's data.
remediation: Implement a centralized authorization middleware that validates the ownership of a resource (e.g., object ID) against the current user's session ID.
Use non-predictable, cryptographically strong identifiers (like UUIDs) for resource handles instead of sequential integer IDs.
Integrate automated DAST tools that specifically test for IDOR by attempting to swap object IDs between different authenticated user contexts.

TECHNOLOGY.question: Are detailed audit logs (API calls, data exports) centralized and stored for 90+ days?
TECHNOLOGY.yes_attribute: Detailed audit logs are centralized and retained for at least 90 days.
TECHNOLOGY.no_attribute: Audit logs are not centralized or are retained for less than 90 days.
remediation: Configure log forwarders (e.g., Fluentd, Logstash) to send all application and system logs to a central log management platform like Splunk or Datadog.
Define and enforce a data retention policy in your logging platform to prevent the automatic deletion of audit logs before the 90-day threshold.
Implement log integrity checks (e.g., digital signing or hashing) to ensure that audit logs cannot be tampered with after they are generated.

TECHNOLOGY.question: Does the product support MFA and enterprise SSO integration (SAML/OIDC)?
TECHNOLOGY.yes_attribute: The product supports multi-factor authentication (MFA) and enterprise SSO (SAML/OIDC).
TECHNOLOGY.no_attribute: The product lacks support for MFA or enterprise SSO integrations.
remediation: Integrate an identity provider (idp) service like Okta, Auth0, or Microsoft Entra ID to support SAML 2.0 or OIDC protocols.
Enforce mandatory MFA (e.g., TOTP or webauthn) for all users, or at a minimum, for all administrative roles within the application.
Provide an 'SSO configuration' portal for enterprise customers to allow them to map their own corporate directories to the product.

TECHNOLOGY.question: Is a dedicated secrets vault (e.g., HashiCorp Vault) used to retrieve credentials at runtime?
TECHNOLOGY.yes_attribute: A dedicated secrets vault is used to retrieve credentials dynamically at runtime.
TECHNOLOGY.no_attribute: Credentials are not managed via a dedicated vault and are likely static.
remediation: Migrate all static secrets from environment variables and config files into a managed vault such as HashiCorp Vault, AWS Secrets Manager, or Azure Key Vault.
Refactor application code to use sdks or 'sidecar' agents that pull secrets from the vault only when they are needed at runtime.
Implement a policy where secrets are never stored on persistent disk and only exist in memory for the duration of the application's process.

TECHNOLOGY.question: Is hardware-level or container-level isolation used for high-risk customer workloads?
TECHNOLOGY.yes_attribute: Hardware or container-level isolation is used for high-risk workloads.
TECHNOLOGY.no_attribute: High-risk workloads do not have dedicated hardware or container-level isolation.
remediation: Deploy high-risk customer workloads into dedicated Kubernetes namespaces with strict pod security policies and network policies.
Utilize micro-VM technology (e.g., Firecracker or gVisor) to provide stronger isolation for untrusted code execution or multi-tenant processing.
Assign high-value or sensitive customers to dedicated 'single-tenant' infrastructure clusters to eliminate the risk of 'noisy neighbor' or cross-tenant leaks.

TECHNOLOGY.question: Are logs analyzed in real-time by a SIEM to detect and block suspicious account activity?
TECHNOLOGY.yes_attribute: Logs are analyzed in real-time by a SIEM to detect and block suspicious activity.
TECHNOLOGY.no_attribute: Logs are not analyzed in real-time for threat detection.
remediation: Establish real-time alerts in your SIEM for anomalous patterns, such as multiple failed logins followed by a successful one from a new IP (brute force).
Integrate automated response actions (SOAR) that can temporarily disable a user account or block an IP address when a high-confidence alert is triggered.
Regularly tune SIEM detection rules using threat intelligence feeds to ensure you are monitoring for current and relevant attack vectors.

TECHNOLOGY.question: Does the product utilize "risk-based" auth to trigger challenges based on unusual behavior?
TECHNOLOGY.yes_attribute: The product utilizes risk-based authentication to trigger security challenges.
TECHNOLOGY.no_attribute: The product does not support risk-based or adaptive authentication.
remediation: Implement an adaptive authentication engine that calculates a 'risk score' for each login based on location, device fingerprint, and time of day.
Configure the system to automatically require a second factor (MFA) only when the login risk score exceeds a specific threshold.
Enable 'impossible travel' detection to flag and block login attempts that occur from geographically distant locations within a short timeframe.

TECHNOLOGY.question: Are short-lived tokens or "dynamic secrets" used, eliminating permanent credentials?
TECHNOLOGY.yes_attribute: Short-lived tokens or dynamic secrets are used instead of permanent credentials.
TECHNOLOGY.no_attribute: The system relies on permanent credentials rather than dynamic secrets.
remediation: Configure your secrets vault to generate 'dynamic' database credentials that expire automatically after a set period (e.g., 4 hours).
Implement just-in-time (JIT) access for administrative tasks, where temporary elevated permissions are granted and then revoked automatically.
Use short-lived OIDC tokens for machine-to-machine authentication to eliminate the need for long-lived static API keys.

SECURITY_AND_RELIABILITY.question: Is there a roadmap that includes security features?
SECURITY_AND_RELIABILITY.yes_attribute: There is a product roadmap that explicitly includes security features and enhancements.
SECURITY_AND_RELIABILITY.no_attribute: The product roadmap does not currently track specific security features.
remediation: Create a dedicated 'security' track or swimlane within the master product roadmap to ensure visibility for security debt and features.
Ensure at least 10-20% of every development cycle (sprint/quarter) is allocated to security-specific enhancements or vulnerability remediation.
Document and share the high-level security roadmap with key internal stakeholders to align security goals with business growth.

SECURITY_AND_RELIABILITY.question: Does the product meet minimum regulatory requirements (GDPR, etc.)?
SECURITY_AND_RELIABILITY.yes_attribute: The product meets minimum regulatory requirements, such as GDPR or relevant industry standards.
SECURITY_AND_RELIABILITY.no_attribute: The product does not yet fully meet minimum regulatory requirements.
remediation: Conduct a formal data protection impact assessment (DPIA) to identify gaps in GDPR compliance or other relevant regional regulations.
Implement a 'privacy by design' framework that includes automated cookie consent management and data subject access request (DSAR) workflows.
Engage a third-party legal or compliance firm to perform an annual audit and provide a gap analysis against target regulatory standards.

SECURITY_AND_RELIABILITY.question: Is there an inventory of all open-source libraries used?
SECURITY_AND_RELIABILITY.yes_attribute: An inventory of all open-source libraries used in the product is maintained.
SECURITY_AND_RELIABILITY.no_attribute: There is no centralized inventory of open-source libraries used.
remediation: Implement a software composition analysis (SCA) tool (e.g., Snyk, FOSSA) to automatically scan and index all third-party dependencies.
Maintain a 'golden repository' of approved open-source libraries and forbid the use of any library not currently in the approved inventory.
Configure automated alerts to notify the security team when a library in the inventory reaches 'end of life' (EOL) or loses community support.

SECURITY_AND_RELIABILITY.question: Is the roadmap reviewed quarterly with cross-functional stakeholders?
SECURITY_AND_RELIABILITY.yes_attribute: The security roadmap is reviewed quarterly with cross-functional stakeholders.
SECURITY_AND_RELIABILITY.no_attribute: There is no quarterly cross-functional review of the security roadmap.
remediation: Establish a recurring quarterly security steering committee involving product, engineering, legal, and sales leadership.
Standardize a reporting template that tracks security roadmap progress, upcoming milestones, and emerging risks to present during reviews.
Implement a formal sign-off process where stakeholders acknowledge the trade-offs made between new features and security investments.

SECURITY_AND_RELIABILITY.question: Is the architecture mapped against a framework like NIST SSDF or ISO 27001?
SECURITY_AND_RELIABILITY.yes_attribute: The architecture is mapped against an industry framework such as NIST SSDF or ISO 27001.
SECURITY_AND_RELIABILITY.no_attribute: The architecture is not formally mapped against a security framework.
remediation: Perform a formal mapping exercise to align current architectural controls with the NIST secure software development framework (SSDF) requirements.
Utilize a GRC (governance, risk, and compliance) platform to track control implementation status against ISO 27001 Annex A controls.
Develop an internal 'control catalog' that bridges technical architecture diagrams to specific compliance framework citations.

SECURITY_AND_RELIABILITY.question: Is a software bill of materials (SBOM) generated for every release?
SECURITY_AND_RELIABILITY.yes_attribute: A software bill of materials (SBOM) is automatically generated for every release.
SECURITY_AND_RELIABILITY.no_attribute: An SBOM is not generated for product releases.
remediation: Integrate an SBOM generation tool (using CycloneDX or SPDX formats) into the final stage of the CI/CD build pipeline.
Store a versioned SBOM alongside every release artifact in your package manager (e.g., Artifactory, Nexus).
Define a process to provide customers with the SBOM upon request or via a secure customer portal to support their own supply chain risk management.

SECURITY_AND_RELIABILITY.question: Is the roadmap dynamically updated based on threat intel and customer feedback loops?
SECURITY_AND_RELIABILITY.yes_attribute: The roadmap is dynamically updated based on threat intelligence and customer feedback loops.
SECURITY_AND_RELIABILITY.no_attribute: The roadmap is static and does not incorporate real-time threat intelligence.
remediation: Establish a 'fast track' process in the roadmap to allow for the immediate insertion of items triggered by zero-day threats or critical intel.
Integrate feedback from the bug bounty program and customer security inquiries directly into the product backlog refinement process.
Utilize threat modeling data from new attack vectors found in the wild to proactively pivot the roadmap toward more resilient architectural patterns.

SECURITY_AND_RELIABILITY.question: Does the product provide "compliance-as-code" templates for customer audits?
SECURITY_AND_RELIABILITY.yes_attribute: The product provides 'compliance-as-code' templates to assist customers with audits.
SECURITY_AND_RELIABILITY.no_attribute: The product does not provide automated compliance templates.
remediation: Develop and publish Terraform or CloudFormation templates that are pre-configured to meet specific compliance standards (e.g., HIPAA-ready).
Provide open policy agent (OPA) bundles that customers can use to automatically audit their own product configurations against your best practices.
Create 'audit packs' containing automated scripts and documentation that map product features to common auditor evidence requests.

SECURITY_AND_RELIABILITY.question: Does a tool automatically block the build if a new high-risk library vulnerability is found?
SECURITY_AND_RELIABILITY.yes_attribute: Automated tools block the build when high-risk library vulnerabilities are detected.
SECURITY_AND_RELIABILITY.no_attribute: High-risk vulnerabilities do not automatically trigger a build block.
remediation: Configure SCA tools to trigger a non-zero exit code in the CI pipeline when a vulnerability exceeds a defined CVSS threshold (e.g., 7.0+).
Implement a 'security exception' workflow where a blocked build can only be bypassed with a documented risk acceptance from the CISO/security lead.
Utilize 'vex' (vulnerability exploitability exchange) files to filter out non-exploitable vulnerabilities from blocking the build, reducing developer friction.

PHYSICAL.question: Is the product hosted in a tier 3+ data center or a major cloud provider (AWS/azure)?
PHYSICAL.yes_attribute: The product is hosted in a tier 3+ data center or a major cloud provider (AWS/azure).
PHYSICAL.no_attribute: The product is not hosted in a tier 3+ data center or major cloud environment.
remediation: Migrate product workloads to a certified tier 3 or tier 4 data center that guarantees 99.98% uptime and redundant power/cooling.
Relocate infrastructure to a major public cloud provider (AWS, azure, GCP) to leverage their global physical security certifications.
Document the specific data center regions and availability zones used, ensuring they meet the organization's uptime and security requirements.

PHYSICAL.question: Are the servers used for the CI/CD pipeline locked and restricted to specific admins?
PHYSICAL.yes_attribute: CI/CD pipeline servers are physically or logically locked and restricted to authorized admins.
PHYSICAL.no_attribute: CI/CD pipeline servers are not sufficiently restricted or locked.
remediation: House on-premises build servers in locked server racks with access restricted to a verified list of system administrators.
Implement strict IAM roles and network security groups (nsgs) to ensure only authorized build-engineering identities can access CI/CD controllers.
Enable 'MFA for SSH' or similar jump-box requirements to prevent unauthorized remote access to the build server operating systems.

PHYSICAL.question: Does the organization perform annual reviews of the host's physical security (SOC 2 Type II)?
PHYSICAL.yes_attribute: Annual reviews of the host's physical security (e.g., SOC 2 Type II) are performed.
PHYSICAL.no_attribute: Annual reviews of the host's physical security are not performed.
remediation: Establish a formal vendor risk management process to collect and review SOC 2 Type II or ISO 27001 reports from all hosting providers annually.
Document the review findings in a centralized GRC tool, specifically noting any physical security 'exceptions' mentioned in the auditor's report.
Require hosting providers to provide a 'bridge letter' for any months not covered by their most recent annual soc2 report to ensure continuous coverage.

PHYSICAL.question: Is access to the physical build office/server room protected by 2fa (badge + biometric)?
PHYSICAL.yes_attribute: Physical access to critical build areas is protected by multi-factor authentication (e.g., badge + biometric).
PHYSICAL.no_attribute: Physical access to build areas does not require multi-factor authentication.
remediation: Upgrade entry points to the server room or build area to require both a physical keycard/badge and a biometric scan (fingerprint or iris).
Implement a 'man-trap' or interlocking door system at the primary entrance of the build office to prevent tailgating.
Review and audit physical access logs monthly to identify and investigate any unauthorized entry attempts or unusual access patterns.

PHYSICAL.question: Are product workloads geofenced to specific regions to meet physical data residency laws?
PHYSICAL.yes_attribute: Product workloads are geofenced to specific regions to comply with data residency laws.
PHYSICAL.no_attribute: Product workloads are not geofenced to specific physical regions.
remediation: Use cloud provider 'region' and 'resource policy' constraints to programmatically prevent data from being stored or processed outside of designated borders.
Implement database sharding or localized instances that ensure customer-specific data remains within the customer's legal jurisdiction.
Configure automated alerts that trigger if data movements or infrastructure provisioning attempts are detected in non-authorized geographic regions.

PHYSICAL.question: Is the build environment "ephemeral," spinning up in a clean state and destroying itself after each build?
PHYSICAL.yes_attribute: The build environment is ephemeral, spinning up in a clean state and destroying itself after each use.
PHYSICAL.no_attribute: The build environment is persistent rather than ephemeral.
remediation: Migrate from persistent build vms to containerized build agents (e.g., Kubernetes runners) that are instantiated for a single job and then purged.
Implement a 'clean room' build strategy where every build starts from a hardened, read-only 'golden image' to ensure no cross-contamination of artifacts.
Automate the post-build 'wipe' process to ensure all temporary files, environment variables, and cached credentials are destroyed immediately upon completion.
